commonIssues:
A very frequent issue is that the land records in the Bhulekh portal do not match the actual physical location, dimensions, or area of the land on the ground.

How to fix:

  • Apply for correction (Dharadhikar) online via the UP Bhulekh portal or approach the local Tehsildar. UP Bhulekh

Many properties suffer from incomplete documentation, such as missing sale deeds or partition deeds, leading to disputes among co-owners.

How to fix:

  • Obtain a certified copy of the Khatauni and apply for partition (Kurra) if it's ancestral property.

Lands marked as "dotted" (government claim) or non-saleable (pond, graveyard) are sometimes illegally put up for sale.

How to fix:

  • Verify land status on the IGRSUP portal before purchase to ensure it's not marked as gram sabha or prohibited. IGRSUP

Property owners often fail to update their name in the revenue records after inheriting land, leading to complex, outdated records.

How to fix:

  • Apply for Virasat (Inheritance Mutation) online via the UP Revenue Court Computerized Management System (RCCMS). RCCMS UP

Sellers often fail to provide necessary No Objection Certificates (NOCs) from departments like Fire, Pollution Control, or local development authorities.

How to fix:

  • Apply for NOCs through the Nivesh Mitra portal or local development authorities. Nivesh Mitra

Cases occur where a person other than the true owner attempts to sell the property, using fraudulent documents.

How to fix:

  • Always verify the seller's Aadhaar and photograph in the SRO database. Report fraud to the local police.

New stricter rules mandate that if the seller's name does not match the Khatauni record, the registration stops immediately.

How to fix:

  • Ensure the seller's name exactly matches the Khatauni before attempting registration. Correct the Khatauni first if mismatched.

Even after the sale deed is registered, the crucial process of updating the government revenue records can take 30–45 days or longer.

How to fix:

  • Track your mutation application online on the UP Bhulekh or e-NagarSewa portal and follow up with the Lekhpal.

Buyers sometimes find that pending property tax dues from the previous owner have transferred to them, causing financial liability.

How to fix:

  • Check and clear all property tax dues on the e-NagarSewa portal before completing the purchase.

For apartment purchases, the absence of an OC from the builder prevents new owners from getting formal electricity or water connections.

How to fix:

  • Request the builder to provide the OC from the local development authority before taking possession.

The EC may fail to reflect the new ownership immediately after registration, leaving the ownership status looking cloudy in the interim.

How to fix:

  • Apply for a manual EC check at the SRO if recent transactions are missing from the IGRSUP portal.

Despite online options, many citizens report having to visit the Tehsildar's office repeatedly for manual verification.

How to fix:

  • Use online grievance portals like Jansunwai to report undue delays or demands. Jansunwai
